title: Prime meridian

infobox.value: is a [meridian](/wiki/Meridian_(geography) "Meridian (geography)") (a line of [longitude](/wiki/Longitude "Longitude")) in a [geographic coordinate system](/wiki/Geographic_coordinate_system "Geographic coordinate system") at which longitude is defined to be 0°. On a spheroid, a prime meridian and its anti-meridian (the [180th meridian](/wiki/180th_meridian "180th meridian") in a [360-degree system](/wiki/Degree_(angle) "Degree (angle)")) form a [great ellipse](/wiki/Great_ellipse "Great ellipse"). This divides the body (e.g. [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth")) into [two hemispheres](/wiki/Hemispheres_of_Earth "Hemispheres of Earth"): the [Eastern Hemisphere](/wiki/Eastern_Hemisphere "Eastern Hemisphere") and the [Western Hemisphere](/wiki/Western_Hemisphere "Western Hemisphere") (for an east-west notational system). Unlike the [equator](/wiki/Equator "Equator"), which also divides a spherical celestial body into two hemispheres, the prime meridian is astronomically arbitrary. For each of Earth's historic prime meridians, various conventions have been used or advocated in different regions throughout history,[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENorgateNorgate2006-1) but none (unlike the [prime meridian of Mars](/wiki/Airy-0 "Airy-0")) have a basis in [physical geography](/wiki/Physical_geography "Physical geography"). Earth's current [international standard](/wiki/International_standard "International standard") prime meridian is the [IERS Reference Meridian](/wiki/IERS_Reference_Meridian "IERS Reference Meridian"). It is derived, but differs slightly, from the [Greenwich Meridian](/wiki/Prime_meridian_(Greenwich) "Prime meridian (Greenwich)"), the previous standard.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Line of longitude, at which longitude is defined to be 0°

"Prime Meridian" redirects here. For Earth's prime meridian, often called the Prime Meridian, see [IERS Reference Meridian](/wiki/IERS_Reference_Meridian "IERS Reference Meridian"). For the historical meridian it is based on, see [Prime meridian (Greenwich)](/wiki/Prime_meridian_(Greenwich) "Prime meridian (Greenwich)").

Countries that touch the [Equator](/wiki/Equator "Equator") (red) and that touch the IERS Reference Meridian (blue)

A **prime meridian** is a [meridian](/wiki/Meridian_(geography) "Meridian (geography)") (a line of [longitude](/wiki/Longitude "Longitude")) in a [geographic coordinate system](/wiki/Geographic_coordinate_system "Geographic coordinate system") at which longitude is defined to be 0°. On a spheroid, a prime meridian and its anti-meridian (the [180th meridian](/wiki/180th_meridian "180th meridian") in a [360-degree system](/wiki/Degree_(angle) "Degree (angle)")) form a [great ellipse](/wiki/Great_ellipse "Great ellipse"). This divides the body (e.g. [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth")) into [two hemispheres](/wiki/Hemispheres_of_Earth "Hemispheres of Earth"): the [Eastern Hemisphere](/wiki/Eastern_Hemisphere "Eastern Hemisphere") and the [Western Hemisphere](/wiki/Western_Hemisphere "Western Hemisphere") (for an east-west notational system). Unlike the [equator](/wiki/Equator "Equator"), which also divides a spherical celestial body into two hemispheres, the prime meridian is astronomically arbitrary. For each of Earth's historic prime meridians, various conventions have been used or advocated in different regions throughout history,[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENorgateNorgate2006-1) but none (unlike the [prime meridian of Mars](/wiki/Airy-0 "Airy-0")) have a basis in [physical geography](/wiki/Physical_geography "Physical geography"). Earth's current [international standard](/wiki/International_standard "International standard") prime meridian is the [IERS Reference Meridian](/wiki/IERS_Reference_Meridian "IERS Reference Meridian"). It is derived, but differs slightly, from the [Greenwich Meridian](/wiki/Prime_meridian_(Greenwich) "Prime meridian (Greenwich)"), the previous standard.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/Atlas_Cosmographicae_%28Mercator%29_033.jpg/330px-Atlas_Cosmographicae_%28Mercator%29_033.jpg)](/wiki/File:Atlas_Cosmographicae_(Mercator)_033.jpg)

[Gerardus Mercator](/wiki/Gerardus_Mercator "Gerardus Mercator") in his *[Atlas Cosmographicae](/wiki/Atlas_Cosmographicae "Atlas Cosmographicae")* (1595) used a prime meridian somewhere close to [25°W](/wiki/25th_meridian_west "25th meridian west"), passing just to the west of [Santa Maria Island](/wiki/Santa_Maria_Island "Santa Maria Island") in the [Azores](/wiki/Azores "Azores") in the [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean"). His 180th meridian runs along the [Strait of Anián](/wiki/Strait_of_Ani%C3%A1n "Strait of Anián") (Bering Strait)

Longitudes for the Earth and [Moon](/wiki/Moon "Moon") are measured from their prime meridian (at 0°) to 180° east and west. For all other [Solar System](/wiki/Solar_System "Solar System") bodies, longitude is measured from 0° (their prime meridian) to 360°. West longitudes are used if the rotation of the body is [prograde](/wiki/Retrograde_and_prograde_motion "Retrograde and prograde motion") (or 'direct', like Earth), meaning that its direction of rotation is the same as that of its orbit. East longitudes are used if the rotation is [retrograde](/wiki/Retrograde_and_prograde_motion "Retrograde and prograde motion").[[3]](#cite_note-cartographic-3)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prime_meridian&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

See also: [History of longitude](/wiki/History_of_longitude "History of longitude")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/Ptolemy-World_Vat_Urb_82.jpg/330px-Ptolemy-World_Vat_Urb_82.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ptolemy-World_Vat_Urb_82.jpg)

[Ptolemy](/wiki/Ptolemy "Ptolemy")'s 1st projection, redrawn under [Maximus Planudes](/wiki/Maximus_Planudes "Maximus Planudes") around 1300, using a prime meridian through the [Canary Islands](/wiki/Canary_Islands "Canary Islands") west of Africa, at the left-hand edge of the map (The obvious central line shown here is the junction of two sheets.)

The notion of longitude for Greeks was developed by the [Greek](/wiki/Greeks "Greeks") [Eratosthenes](/wiki/Eratosthenes "Eratosthenes") (c. 276 – 195 BCE) in [Alexandria](/wiki/Alexandria "Alexandria"), and [Hipparchus](/wiki/Hipparchus "Hipparchus") (c. 190 – 120 BCE) in [Rhodes](/wiki/Rhodes "Rhodes"), and applied to a large number of cities by the [geographer](/wiki/Geographer "Geographer") [Strabo](/wiki/Strabo "Strabo") (64/63 BCE – c. 24 CE). [Ptolemy](/wiki/Ptolemy "Ptolemy") (c. 90 – 168 CE) was the first geographer to use a consistent meridian for a world map, in his *[Geographia](/wiki/Geography_(Ptolemy) "Geography (Ptolemy)")*.

Ptolemy used as his basis the "[Fortunate Isles](/wiki/Fortunate_Isles "Fortunate Isles")", a group of islands in the [Atlantic](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean"), which are usually associated with the [Canary Islands](/wiki/Canary_Islands "Canary Islands") (13°W to 18°W), although his maps correspond more closely to the [Cape Verde islands](/wiki/Cape_Verde "Cape Verde") (22°W to 25°W). The main point is to be comfortably west of the western tip of [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa") (17°30′W) as negative numbers were not yet in use. His prime meridian corresponds to 18°40′ west of [Winchester](/wiki/Winchester "Winchester") (about 20°W) today.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENorgateNorgate2006-1) At that time the chief method of determining longitude was by using the reported times of [lunar eclipses](/wiki/Lunar_eclipse "Lunar eclipse") in different countries.

One of the earliest known descriptions of *standard time* in India appeared in the 4th century CE [astronomical](/wiki/Hindu_astronomy "Hindu astronomy") treatise [Surya Siddhanta](/wiki/Surya_Siddhanta "Surya Siddhanta"). Postulating a [spherical Earth](/wiki/Spherical_Earth "Spherical Earth"), the book described the thousands years old customs of the [prime meridian](/wiki/Prime_Meridian "Prime Meridian"), or zero longitude, as passing through *[Avanti](/wiki/Avanti_(India) "Avanti (India)")*, the ancient name for the historic city of [Ujjain](/wiki/Ujjain "Ujjain") in Central India, and *Rohitaka*, the ancient name for [Rohtak](/wiki/Rohtak "Rohtak") ([28°54′N 76°38′E﻿ / ﻿28.900°N 76.633°E﻿ / 28.900; 76.633﻿ (Rohitaka (Rohtak))](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Prime_meridian&params=28_54_N_76_38_E_type:city&title=Rohitaka+%28Rohtak%29)), a city 750 km (470 mi) north.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/Propaganda_Map.jpg/500px-Propaganda_Map.jpg)](/wiki/File:Propaganda_Map.jpg)

[William Grigg](/wiki/William_Griggs_(inventor) "William Griggs (inventor)")'s facsimile of the 1529 [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_Empire "Spanish Empire") [Padron Real](/wiki/Padron_Real "Padron Real"), from the copy made by [Diogo Ribeiro](/wiki/Diogo_Ribeiro_(cartographer) "Diogo Ribeiro (cartographer)") and held by the [Vatican Library](/wiki/Vatican_Library "Vatican Library")

Ptolemy's *Geographia* was first printed with maps at [Bologna](/wiki/Bologna "Bologna") in 1477, and many early globes in the 16th century followed his lead, but there was still a hope that a "natural" basis for a prime meridian existed. In 1493, [Christopher Columbus](/wiki/Christopher_Columbus "Christopher Columbus") reported that the compass pointed due north somewhere in mid-Atlantic, and this fact was used in the important [Treaty of Tordesillas](/wiki/Treaty_of_Tordesillas "Treaty of Tordesillas") of 1494, which settled the territorial dispute between [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain") and [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal "Portugal") over newly discovered lands. The Tordesillas line was eventually settled at 370 [leagues](/wiki/League_(unit) "League (unit)") (about 2,190 kilometres (1,360 miles; 1,180 nautical miles)) west of [Cape Verde](/wiki/Cape_Verde "Cape Verde").[[a]](#cite_note-8) This is shown in the copies of Spain's [Padron Real](/wiki/Padron_Real "Padron Real") made by [Diogo Ribeiro](/wiki/Diogo_Ribeiro_(cartographer) "Diogo Ribeiro (cartographer)") in 1527 and 1529. [São Miguel Island](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Miguel_Island "São Miguel Island") (25°30′W) in the [Azores](/wiki/Azores "Azores") was still used for the same reason as late as 1594 by [Christopher Saxton](/wiki/Christopher_Saxton "Christopher Saxton"), although by then it had been shown that the zero [magnetic declination](/wiki/Magnetic_declination "Magnetic declination") line did not follow a line of longitude.[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHooker2006-9)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/Atlas_Ortelius_KB_PPN369376781-006av-006br.jpg/330px-Atlas_Ortelius_KB_PPN369376781-006av-006br.jpg)](/wiki/File:Atlas_Ortelius_KB_PPN369376781-006av-006br.jpg)

1571 Africa map by [Abraham Ortelius](/wiki/Abraham_Ortelius "Abraham Ortelius"), with [Cape Verde](/wiki/Cape_Verde "Cape Verde") marking its prime meridian

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/CEM-36-Regno-della-China-2355.jpg/330px-CEM-36-Regno-della-China-2355.jpg)](/wiki/File:CEM-36-Regno-della-China-2355.jpg)

1682 map of East Asia by [Giacomo Cantelli](/wiki/Giacomo_Cantelli "Giacomo Cantelli"), with Cape Verde originating its prime meridian; [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") is thus located around 180° E.

In 1541, [Mercator](/wiki/Gerardus_Mercator "Gerardus Mercator") produced his 41 cm terrestrial globe and drew his prime meridian precisely through [Fuerteventura](/wiki/Fuerteventura "Fuerteventura") (14°1′W) in the Canaries. His later maps used the Azores, following the magnetic hypothesis, but by the time that [Ortelius](/wiki/Abraham_Ortelius "Abraham Ortelius") produced the first modern atlas in 1570, other islands such as Cape Verde were coming into use. In his atlas longitudes were counted from 0° to 360°, not 180°W to 180°E as is usual today. This practice was followed by navigators well into the 18th century.[[9]](#cite_note-10) In 1634, [Cardinal Richelieu](/wiki/Cardinal_Richelieu "Cardinal Richelieu") used the westernmost island of the Canaries, [El Hierro](/wiki/El_Hierro "El Hierro"), 19°55′ west of Paris, as the choice of meridian. The geographer [Delisle](/wiki/Guillaume_Delisle "Guillaume Delisle") decided to round this off to 20°, so that it simply became the meridian of Paris disguised.[[10]](#cite_note-11)

In the early 18th century, the battle was on to improve the determination of longitude at sea, leading to the development of the [marine chronometer](/wiki/Marine_chronometer "Marine chronometer") by [John Harrison](/wiki/John_Harrison "John Harrison"). The development of accurate star charts, principally by the first British [Astronomer Royal](/wiki/Astronomer_Royal "Astronomer Royal"), [John Flamsteed](/wiki/John_Flamsteed "John Flamsteed") between 1680 and 1719 and disseminated by his successor [Edmund Halley](/wiki/Edmund_Halley "Edmund Halley"), enabled navigators to use the [lunar method](/wiki/Lunar_distance_(navigation) "Lunar distance (navigation)") of determining longitude more accurately using the [octant](/wiki/Octant_(instrument) "Octant (instrument)") developed by [Thomas Godfrey](/wiki/Thomas_Godfrey_(inventor) "Thomas Godfrey (inventor)") and [John Hadley](/wiki/John_Hadley "John Hadley").[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESobelAndrewes1998110–115-12)

In the 18th century most countries in [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe") adapted their own *prime* meridian, usually through their capital, hence in [France](/wiki/France "France") the [Paris meridian](/wiki/Paris_meridian "Paris meridian") was prime, in [Prussia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Prussia "Kingdom of Prussia") it was the [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin") meridian, in [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark") the [Copenhagen](/wiki/Copenhagen "Copenhagen") meridian, and in [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") the [Greenwich meridian](/wiki/Greenwich_meridian "Greenwich meridian").

Between 1765 and 1811, [Nevil Maskelyne](/wiki/Nevil_Maskelyne "Nevil Maskelyne") published 49 issues of the [*Nautical Almanac*](/wiki/The_Nautical_Almanac "The Nautical Almanac") based on the meridian of the [Royal Observatory, Greenwich](/wiki/Royal_Observatory,_Greenwich "Royal Observatory, Greenwich"). "Maskelyne's tables not only made the lunar method practicable, they also made the [Greenwich meridian](/wiki/Prime_meridian_(Greenwich) "Prime meridian (Greenwich)") the universal reference point. Even the French translations of the *Nautical Almanac* retained Maskelyne's calculations from Greenwich – in spite of the fact that every other table in the *[Connaissance des Temps](/wiki/Connaissance_des_Temps "Connaissance des Temps")* considered the [Paris meridian](/wiki/Paris_meridian "Paris meridian") as the prime."[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESobelAndrewes1998197–199-13)

In 1884, at the [International Meridian Conference](/wiki/International_Meridian_Conference "International Meridian Conference") in [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."), 22 countries voted to adopt the Greenwich meridian as the prime meridian of the world.[[13]](#cite_note-who_decided-14) The French argued for a neutral line, mentioning the Azores and the [Bering Strait](/wiki/Bering_Strait "Bering Strait"), but eventually abstained and continued to use the [Paris meridian](/wiki/Paris_meridian "Paris meridian") until 1911.

The current international standard Prime Meridian is the [IERS Reference Meridian](/wiki/IERS_Reference_Meridian "IERS Reference Meridian"). The [International Hydrographic Organization](/wiki/International_Hydrographic_Organization "International Hydrographic Organization") adopted an early version of the IRM in 1983 for all nautical charts.[[14]](#cite_note-15) It was adopted for air navigation by the [International Civil Aviation Organization](/wiki/International_Civil_Aviation_Organization "International Civil Aviation Organization") on 3 March 1989.[[15]](#cite_note-16)

## International prime meridian

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prime_meridian&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: International prime meridian")]

Since 1984, the international standard for the Earth's prime meridian is the IERS Reference Meridian. Between 1884 and 1984, the meridian of Greenwich was the world standard. These meridians are very close to each other.

### Prime meridian at Greenwich

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prime_meridian&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Prime meridian at Greenwich")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Prime_meridian.jpg/330px-Prime_meridian.jpg)](/wiki/File:Prime_meridian.jpg)

The line of the [Greenwich meridian](/wiki/Prime_meridian_(Greenwich) "Prime meridian (Greenwich)") at the [Royal Observatory, Greenwich](/wiki/Royal_Observatory,_Greenwich "Royal Observatory, Greenwich"), England

Main article: [Prime meridian (Greenwich)](/wiki/Prime_meridian_(Greenwich) "Prime meridian (Greenwich)")

In October 1884 the Greenwich Meridian was selected by delegates (forty-one delegates representing twenty-five nations) to the [International Meridian Conference](/wiki/International_Meridian_Conference "International Meridian Conference") held in [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."), [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") to be the common zero of longitude and standard of time reckoning throughout the world.[[16]](#cite_note-imc-17)[[b]](#cite_note-19)

The position of the historic prime meridian, based at the [Royal Observatory, Greenwich](/wiki/Royal_Observatory,_Greenwich "Royal Observatory, Greenwich"), was established by [Sir George Airy](/wiki/George_Biddell_Airy "George Biddell Airy") in 1851. It was defined by the location of the [Airy Transit Circle](/wiki/Meridian_circle#19th_century "Meridian circle") ever since the first observation he took with it.[[18]](#cite_note-Greenwich_Observatory_p.10-20) Prior to that, it was defined by a succession of earlier transit instruments, the first of which was acquired by the second [Astronomer Royal](/wiki/Astronomer_Royal "Astronomer Royal"), [Edmond Halley](/wiki/Edmond_Halley "Edmond Halley") in 1721. It was set up in the extreme north-west corner of the Observatory between Flamsteed House and the Western Summer House. This spot, now subsumed into Flamsteed House, is roughly 43 metres (47 yards) to the west of the Airy Transit Circle, a distance equivalent to roughly 2 seconds of longitude.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDolan2013a-21) It was Airy's transit circle that was adopted in principle (with French delegates, who pressed for adoption of the [Paris meridian](/wiki/Paris_meridian "Paris meridian") abstaining) as the Prime Meridian of the world at the 1884 International Meridian Conference.[[20]](#cite_note-Time-22)[[21]](#cite_note-RMG-23)

All of these Greenwich meridians were located via an astronomic observation from the surface of the Earth, oriented via a [plumb line](/wiki/Plumb_line "Plumb line") along the direction of gravity at the surface. This astronomic Greenwich meridian was disseminated around the world, first via the [lunar distance method](/wiki/Lunar_distance_method "Lunar distance method"), then by chronometers carried on ships, then via telegraph lines carried by [submarine communications cables](/wiki/Submarine_communications_cable "Submarine communications cable"), then via radio time signals. One remote longitude ultimately based on the Greenwich meridian using these methods was that of the [North American Datum 1927](/wiki/North_American_Datum#North_American_Datum_of_1927 "North American Datum") or NAD27, an ellipsoid whose surface best matches [mean sea level](/wiki/Mean_sea_level "Mean sea level") under the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States").

### IERS Reference Meridian

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prime_meridian&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: IERS Reference Meridian")]

Main article: [IERS Reference Meridian](/wiki/IERS_Reference_Meridian "IERS Reference Meridian")

Beginning in 1973 the [International Time Bureau](/wiki/International_Time_Bureau "International Time Bureau") and later the [International Earth Rotation and Reference Systems Service](/wiki/International_Earth_Rotation_and_Reference_Systems_Service "International Earth Rotation and Reference Systems Service") changed from reliance on optical instruments like the Airy Transit Circle to techniques such as [lunar laser ranging](/wiki/Lunar_Laser_Ranging_experiment "Lunar Laser Ranging experiment"), [satellite laser ranging](/wiki/Satellite_laser_ranging "Satellite laser ranging"), and [very-long-baseline interferometry](/wiki/Very-long-baseline_interferometry "Very-long-baseline interferometry"). The new techniques resulted in the IERS Reference Meridian, the plane of which passes through the centre of mass of the Earth. This differs from the plane established by the Airy transit, which is affected by [vertical deflection](/wiki/Vertical_deflection "Vertical deflection") (the local vertical is affected by influences such as nearby mountains). The change from relying on the local vertical to using a meridian based on the centre of the Earth caused the modern prime meridian to be [5.3″](/wiki/Minute_and_second_of_arc#Symbols_and_abbreviations "Minute and second of arc") east of the astronomic Greenwich prime meridian through the Airy Transit Circle. At the latitude of Greenwich, this amounts to 102 metres (112 yards).[[22]](#cite_note-24) This was officially accepted by the [Bureau International de l'Heure](/wiki/Bureau_International_de_l%27Heure "Bureau International de l'Heure") (BIH) in 1984 via its BTS84 (BIH Terrestrial System) that later became WGS84 (World Geodetic System 1984) and the various [International Terrestrial Reference Frames](/wiki/International_Terrestrial_Reference_Frame "International Terrestrial Reference Frame") (ITRFs).

Due to the movement of Earth's [tectonic plates](/wiki/Plate_tectonics "Plate tectonics"), the line of 0° longitude along the surface of the Earth has slowly moved toward the west from this shifted position by a few centimetres; that is, towards the Airy Transit Circle (or the Airy Transit Circle has moved toward the east, depending on your point of view) since 1984 (or the 1960s). With the introduction of satellite technology, it became possible to create a more accurate and detailed global map. With these advances there also arose the necessity to define a reference meridian that, whilst being derived from the Airy Transit Circle, would also take into account the effects of plate movement and variations in the way that the Earth was spinning.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDolan2013b-25)
As a result, the [IERS Reference Meridian](/wiki/IERS_Reference_Meridian "IERS Reference Meridian") was established and is commonly used to [denote](/wiki/Denotation_(semiotics) "Denotation (semiotics)") the Earth's prime meridian (0° longitude) by the [International Earth Rotation and Reference Systems Service](/wiki/International_Earth_Rotation_and_Reference_Systems_Service "International Earth Rotation and Reference Systems Service"), which defines and maintains the link between longitude and time. Based on observations to satellites and celestial compact radio sources (quasars) from various coordinated stations around the globe, Airy's [transit circle](/wiki/Transit_circle "Transit circle") drifts northeast about 2.5 centimetres (1 inch) per year relative to this Earth-centred 0° longitude.

It is also the reference meridian of the [Global Positioning System](/wiki/Global_Positioning_System "Global Positioning System") operated by the [United States Department of Defense](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Defense "United States Department of Defense"), and of [WGS84](/wiki/WGS84 "WGS84") and its two formal versions, the ideal [International Terrestrial Reference System](/wiki/International_Terrestrial_Reference_System "International Terrestrial Reference System") (ITRS) and its realization, the [International Terrestrial Reference Frame](/wiki/International_Terrestrial_Reference_Frame "International Terrestrial Reference Frame") (ITRF).[[24]](#cite_note-GPS-26)[[25]](#cite_note-IRM-27)[[c]](#cite_note-28) A current convention on the Earth uses the line of longitude 180° opposite the IRM as the basis for the [International Date Line](/wiki/International_Date_Line "International Date Line").

## Prime meridian on other celestial bodies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prime_meridian&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Prime meridian on other celestial bodies")]

See also: [Longitude (planets)](/wiki/Longitude_(planets) "Longitude (planets)")

"Prime meridian (planets)" redirects here; not to be confused with [Central meridian (planets)](/wiki/Central_meridian_(planets) "Central meridian (planets)").

As on the Earth, prime meridians must be arbitrarily defined. Often a landmark such as a crater is used; other times a prime meridian is defined by reference to another celestial object, or by [magnetic fields](/wiki/Magnetic_fields "Magnetic fields").
The prime meridians of the following planetographic systems have been defined:

* Two different [heliographic coordinate systems](/wiki/Solar_coordinate_systems#Heliographic "Solar coordinate systems") are used on the Sun. The first is the Carrington heliographic coordinate system. In this system, the prime meridian passes through the center of the solar disk as seen from the Earth on 9 November 1853, which is when the English astronomer [Richard Christopher Carrington](/wiki/Richard_Christopher_Carrington "Richard Christopher Carrington") started his observations of [sunspots](/wiki/Sunspots "Sunspots").[[26]](#cite_note-29) The second is the [Stonyhurst heliographic coordinates](/wiki/Stonyhurst_Observatory#Stonyhurst_heliographic_coordinates "Stonyhurst Observatory") system, originated at [Stonyhurst Observatory](/wiki/Stonyhurst_Observatory "Stonyhurst Observatory") in [Lancashire](/wiki/Lancashire "Lancashire"), England.
* In 1975 the prime meridian of [Mercury](/wiki/Mercury_(planet) "Mercury (planet)") was defined[[27]](#cite_note-30)[[28]](#cite_note-31) to be 20° east of the crater [Hun Kal](/wiki/Hun_Kal_(crater) "Hun Kal (crater)").[[29]](#cite_note-IAU09-32) This meridian was chosen because it runs through the point on Mercury's equator where the average temperature is highest (due to the planet's rotation and orbit, the sun briefly [retrogrades](/wiki/Apparent_retrograde_motion "Apparent retrograde motion") at noon at this point during [perihelion](/wiki/Perihelion "Perihelion"), giving it more sunlight).[[30]](#cite_note-33)[[31]](#cite_note-ArchinalA’Hearn2010-34)[[32]](#cite_note-usgs-35)
* Defined in 1992,[[33]](#cite_note-36) the prime meridian of [Venus](/wiki/Venus "Venus") passes through the central peak in [the crater Ariadne](/wiki/Ariadne_(crater) "Ariadne (crater)"), chosen arbitrarily.[[34]](#cite_note-37)
* The prime meridian of the [Moon](/wiki/Moon "Moon") lies directly in the middle of the face of the Moon visible from Earth and passes near the crater [Bruce](/wiki/Bruce_(crater) "Bruce (crater)").[[35]](#cite_note-38)
* The prime meridian of [Mars](/wiki/Mars "Mars") was established in 1971[[36]](#cite_note-39) and passes through the center of the crater [Airy-0](/wiki/Airy-0 "Airy-0"), although it is fixed by the longitude of the [*Viking 1* lander](/wiki/Viking_1#Lander "Viking 1"), which is defined to be 47.95137°W.[[37]](#cite_note-40)
* The prime meridian on [Ceres](/wiki/Ceres_(dwarf_planet) "Ceres (dwarf planet)") runs through the Kait crater, which was arbitrarily chosen because it is near the equator (about 2° south).[[38]](#cite_note-41)
* The prime meridian on [4 Vesta](/wiki/4_Vesta "4 Vesta") is 4 degrees east of the crater [Claudia](/wiki/Claudia_(crater) "Claudia (crater)"), chosen because it is sharply defined.[[39]](#cite_note-IAUcoordinates-42)
* [Jupiter](/wiki/Jupiter "Jupiter") has several coordinate systems because its cloud tops—the only part of the planet visible from space—rotate at different rates depending on latitude.[[40]](#cite_note-43) It is unknown whether Jupiter has any internal solid surface that would enable a more Earth-like coordinate system. System I and System II coordinates are based on atmospheric rotation, and System III coordinates use Jupiter's magnetic field. The prime meridians of Jupiter's four [Galilean moons](/wiki/Galilean_moons "Galilean moons") were established in 1979.[[41]](#cite_note-44)
  + [Europa](/wiki/Europa_(moon) "Europa (moon)")'s prime meridian is defined such that [the crater Cilix](/wiki/Cilix_(crater) "Cilix (crater)") is at 182° W.[[29]](#cite_note-IAU09-32) The 0° longitude runs through the middle of the face that is always turned towards Jupiter.
  + [Io](/wiki/Io_(moon) "Io (moon)")'s prime meridian, like that of Earth's moon, is defined so that it runs through the middle of the face that is always turned towards Jupiter (the near side, known as the subjovian hemisphere).[[42]](#cite_note-Lopes2005-45)
  + [Ganymede](/wiki/Ganymede_(moon) "Ganymede (moon)")'s prime meridian is defined such that [the crater Anat](/w/index.php?title=Anat_(crater)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Anat (crater) (page does not exist)") is at 128° W, and the 0° longitude runs through the middle of the subjovian hemisphere.[[43]](#cite_note-iau.table2-46)
  + [Callisto](/wiki/Callisto_(moon) "Callisto (moon)")'s prime meridian is defined such that [the crater Saga](/w/index.php?title=Saga_(crater)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Saga (crater) (page does not exist)") is at 326° W.[[44]](#cite_note-47)
* [Titan](/wiki/Titan_(moon) "Titan (moon)") is the largest moon of [Saturn](/wiki/Saturn "Saturn") and, like the Earth's moon, is tidally locked and always has the same face towards Saturn. The middle of that face is 0 longitude.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* Like Jupiter, [Neptune](/wiki/Neptune "Neptune") is a gas giant, so any surface is obscured by clouds. The prime meridian of its largest moon, [Triton](/wiki/Triton_(moon) "Triton (moon)"), was established in 1991.[[45]](#cite_note-48)
* [Pluto](/wiki/Pluto "Pluto")'s prime meridian is defined as the meridian passing through the center of the face that is always towards [Charon](/wiki/Charon_(moon) "Charon (moon)"), its largest moon, as the two are tidally locked to each other. Charon's prime meridian is similarly defined as the meridian always facing directly toward Pluto.

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prime_meridian&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-8)**  These figures use the *legua náutica* (nautical league) of four [Roman miles](/wiki/Roman_mile "Roman mile") totalling 5.926 km (3.682 mi), which was used by Spain during the 15th, 16th, and 17th centuries for navigation. Given the imprecision of the source, the calculation has been rounded to three significant digits.[[5]](#cite_note-5) In 1897 Henry Harrise noted that Jaime Ferrer, the expert consulted by King Ferdinand and Queen Isabella, stated that a league was four miles of six [stades](/wiki/Stadion_(unit_of_length) "Stadion (unit of length)") each.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Modern scholars agree that the geographic stade was the Roman or Italian stade, not any of several other Greek stades, supporting these figures.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Harrise is in the minority when he uses the stade of 192.27 m (630.8 ft) marked within the stadium at [Olympia, Greece](/wiki/Olympia,_Greece "Olympia, Greece"), resulting in a league (32 stades) of 6.153 km (3.823 mi), 3.8% larger.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-19)** Voting took place on 13 October and the resolutions were adopted on 22 October 1884.[[17]](#cite_note-18) The modern prime meridian, the IERS Reference Meridian, is placed very near this meridian.[[13]](#cite_note-who_decided-14)
3. **[^](#cite_ref-28)** The astronomic latitude of the Royal Observatory is 51°28′38″N whereas its latitude on the European Terrestrial Reference Frame (1989) [datum](/wiki/Datum_(geodesy) "Datum (geodesy)") is 51°28′40.1247″N.
4. **[^](#cite_ref-68)** When Tolhopff handed over his book, titled *Stellarium* (1480),[[62]](#cite_note-65) to King Matthias Corvinus, he emphasized that he had used the meridian of [Buda](/wiki/Buda "Buda") for his calculations. The German physician, Johannes Müntz used it the same way in his 1495 calendar. However, in the second edition, he used the Vienna meridian.[[63]](#cite_note-66)[[64]](#cite_note-67)
